(21) The Frontend Engineer Forced to Eat Mango - Mocha - Unit Testing

So far we've practiced a fair amount of MongoDB and Mongoose syntax. But in real development, is it actually realistic to just write code straight up? Of course not. If you write it blind, you usually end up burning a ton of time testing it by hand. That's why we reach for tools to do the testing for us, and from this chapter on we'll start practicing with Mocha.

So what's Mocha? In short, it's a JavaScript testing framework. In the Node.js dev ecosystem, most people reach for Mocha when they want to do unit testing. There are other frameworks too, like Jest, but here I'm going with Mocha since more people use it.
So what is unit testing? There are plenty of test types out there: integration testing, unit testing, end-to-end, and so on.
End-to-end is E2E Testing, and E2E might feel more familiar to a frontend developer, because E2E is basically testing that simulates user behavior.
Integration testing is exactly what the name suggests: testing after various pieces are integrated together, making sure everything works as a whole.
And the unit testing this post is about? Unit testing is also called module testing. When you look it up, you'll usually run into a line like this:

A program unit is the smallest testable part of an application. In procedural programming, a unit could be an entire module, but it's more commonly an individual function or procedure. In object-oriented programming, the smallest unit is a method, including methods in a base class (superclass), abstract class, or derived class (subclass).
Basically, you test at the smallest possible unit, and a single unit test should only cover one Class.
We're not here to deep-dive into unit testing itself, just to get a rough lay of the land. So go ahead and install Mocha in your project with this npm command:
language-cmd shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast

Once it's installed, we'll start messing around with Mocha a bit in the next chapter, and after that we'll use Mocha to repeatedly drill the Mongoose syntax from earlier. So this chapter is a light one. Just get Mocha installed and you're good.

We've practiced a ton of MongoDB and Mongoose syntax so far, but is it actually realistic to just write code straight up in real-world development? Of course not. Writing it blind means spending forever testing it by hand, so we lean on tools to test for us. From this chapter on, we'll start playing with Mocha.
